data.title: SILJ: Silver Miners Likely To Outperform In 2026
data.description:
Overview

Most of my Seeking Alpha articles are on undervalued equities, but I do occasionally write about ETFs as well when the setup is compelling enough. Presently, I think the risk reward is especially compelling for silver mining stocks, and the Amplify Junior Silver Miners ETF (SILJ) is a good option for investors that are looking for more broad-based exposure to silver miners.

This ETF is comprised of mining companies with material exposure to silver production or exploration. The minimum market cap of a company included in the ETF is $20M and the chart below highlights the 10 largest holdings in this ETF.

The stock price performance of SILJ has been very impressive over the last year, up a massive 196%, primarily due to the strong performance of silver over this period, which is up 166%. However, if we zoom out and look at the last five years, we can see that SILJ has underperformed silver by quite a bit, up “only” 110% while the price of silver is up as much as 215% over this period.

One potential reason for this underperformance is because few silver miners are pure-play silver producers and most holdings in this ETF either have gold or base metals' exposure as well, which haven't performed quite as well as silver. This might be a somewhat valid argument for base metals producers, but we can see below that gold has outperformed SILJ over the last five years as well.

Metals Vs. Mining Performance

Over a long enough period, I do think gold and silver prices have a decent chance of outperforming many mining companies due to cost inflation, equity dilution, and less than stellar management decisions. However, during a bull market, we frequently see the mining companies outperform, sometimes with a 2-3x multiple.

This is something we saw for the gold miners during the 2000s decade, and gold miners have over the last year outperformed gold rather substantially as well.

SILJ was launched in late 2012, so we have less history to look at for the ETF and silver mining companies in general. With that said, we did see the silver price jump by about 40% in the first half of 2016, following a prolonged bear market in precious metals and the miners after the 2011 peak.

Both precious metals and the precious metals miners were rather depressed at that point, which means it is far from a perfect comparison to today. However, we did see SILJ spiking up close to 300% on that 40% price increase in silver during the first half of 2016. So, I do think it is fair to assume silver miners can offer substantial leverage to the price of silver, even if there are few pure-play silver mining companies in SILJ.

Silver Price

Silver is both a precious and an industrial metal, which has, according to the Silver Institute, been in a deficit for five years now. The industrial demand is due to the conductive properties of silver and its use in solar energy and other electrical products. This demand growth doesn't look to be slowing in the near-term, even if there will always be some industrial substitution and demand destruction the higher the silver price goes.

The demand for gold is to a large degree boosted by central banks, which doesn't apply for silver to the same degree. However, investor demand outside of central banks has been relatively strong for silver over the last few years, especially in Asia, where investors have a more recent memory of currency debasements.

There is little doubt in my mind that we are now in an environment with increased geopolitical turbulence and currency debasements. There is simply no way debts can be honored in real terms, in most Western countries, where the debt to GDP is in the 300-400% range, if we include both government and private sector debt.

I don't have any predictions about the silver price in the near-term, but I do think we are likely to see higher precious metals prices for longer.

Conclusion

Silver mining companies, where SILJ is a good proxy, are priced as if silver prices will retrace substantially over the coming years. I think the silver price has a good chance of either spiking higher from here or stabilizing above $50/oz, which likely means further upside for SILJ going forward.

There is of course the risk that we either see the silver price collapse by itself or that we have more of a broad-based deflationary event, which would very likely cause the price of SILJ to drop from here. Silver mining companies are extremely volatile, both to the upside and the downside, so it is important to adjust position sizes accordingly.

I don't own SILJ directly, as I primarily look for undervalued and/or quality companies within a specific industry, but I own a handful of companies which are holdings in this ETF and I think SILJ is a good proxy for silver mining exposure. I have been increasing my exposure to silver mining companies lately, while scaling back my gold mining exposure some.

data.title: The One Chart That Signals A Major Market Shift Could Be Coming
data.description:
Ever since the beginning of 2023, mega-cap tech stocks have dominated the market's returns (SPY), while dividend stocks (SCHD) have lagged badly.

In particular, REITs (VNQ) have underperformed significantly since the beginning of 2022, and as I detailed recently, even a recent pivot to rate cuts by the Fed has failed to ignite a durable REIT rally.

While REITs continue to face material headwinds, there is one silver lining that indicates there is significant value in the space that can be had if discerning investors can find the right opportunities. That ratio is the S&P 500 (SPX) price-to-earnings multiple relative to the REIT price-to-FFO multiple. In this article, I will detail why this ratio signals that better times could be ahead for dividend investors.

The One Chart Most Income Investors Are Ignoring

At about 1.3 times today, this ratio has reached a level that historically has marked a major inflection point for income-producing real assets. In fact, this level has only been reached two other times in recorded history: the 2008–2009 Great Financial Crisis and the March 2020 COVID Shock.

Effectively, what this means is that investors are paying a 30% premium for general equity earnings relative to earnings that come from highly contracted cash flows from real asset businesses. Historically, this has reflected maximum pessimism toward rate-sensitive, income-generating real assets. It also comes as a result of several years in a row of REITs underperforming the S&P 500, and 2025 marked the widest performance gap yet between REITs and the S&P 500.

This was not driven by collapsing underlying fundamentals in many REITs. In fact, REITs in aggregate saw a 6.2% increase in FFO and a 6.3% increase in total dividends paid year-over-year through the first 3 quarters of 2025. Rather, it was driven by interest rates and significant valuation multiple expansion in the S&P 500, along with valuation multiple contraction among many REITs.

What this indicates is that this is not a sustainable trend, as valuation multiple expansion and contraction can only go on for so long, and true long-term market performance is ultimately driven by growing underlying earnings and intrinsic value.

Why Public REIT Valuations Make Little Sense

Public REITs are materially undervalued right now, as public REITs and implied cap rates have been persistently above private market valuations for the longest stretch since the early 2000s.

This makes little sense because public REITs offer many advantages that private real estate does not, namely liquidity and divisibility, which are perhaps the two biggest weaknesses for private real estate.

Additionally, they generally enjoy access to cheaper costs of capital, far greater operating efficiencies due to economies of scale, superior operating and value-add expertise, and generally better deal flow access due to experience, relationships, and exposure that the management teams provide relative to an individual investor or even a small fund that invests in private real estate.

The only real con of owning publicly traded real estate is that you are exposed to greater market volatility. However, for a long-term-oriented value investor, volatility is actually a feature rather than a bug because it enables you to buy real estate at lower prices than you would be able to get anywhere else, and then you can hold until the market prices it fairly or even ideally prices it at a premium to what it can be purchased for in the private markets.

This means that either the private markets need to see material drawdowns in valuations to where public markets are traded today, or the public markets need to re-rate. Either option gives public real estate a margin of safety relative to private market real estate, and given that the fundamentals are still quite strong in many public market REITs, such as these REITs, it would seem like, at least in some cases, it's public markets that should re-rate higher rather than private markets that should re-rate lower.

High Yields Change the Math

Not only that, but REITs and other real asset businesses like midstream infrastructure (AMLP) and utilities (XLU) all offer yields ranging between 3% and 9% at the moment, which is far greater than what the S&P 500 offers right now at just a little over 1% dividend yield.

This means that these industries need far less growth and valuation-multiple expansion to support the same returns that the S&P 500 could generate. This is especially relevant given that all three of these industries continue to grow. In particular, utilities are benefiting from AI-driven electricity demand, and midstream is benefiting from a combination of data center natural gas-driven demand, as well as re-industrialization and energy export tailwinds.

While REITs lack such dynamic tailwinds in many cases, triple-net lease REITs like Realty Income (O) still have contractual lease escalators and continue to engage in a healthy acquisition pipeline. Moreover, W. P. Carey (WPC) just delivered very strong acquisition results this past year, signaling strong growth still being had in the triple-net lease space. Thus, on top of their attractive yields, these three sectors are still growing at paces that are meeting or even beating inflation over time.

Investor Takeaway

There are plenty of REITs and other dividend-paying stocks (especially traditional consumer goods and industrial dividend growth stocks, as I recently detailed here) that are still facing material headwinds. However, I think if you can buy high-quality triple-net lease REITs on a value basis, such as O, WPC, and National Retail Properties (NNN), as well as midstream MLPs and some other high-quality utility (UTG) and infrastructure (UTF) businesses right now on a value basis, you are set to enjoy very favorable risk-adjusted returns moving forward.

This is due to their high yields that stand in the mid-to-high single digits, strong inflation-beating contractual growth rates due to inflation-linked and/or fixed annual escalators, high mandatory payouts with triple-net lease REITs having to pay out at least 90% of their taxable income each year, and midstream MLPs, utilities, and many other infrastructure businesses having long-duration fee-based contracted and/or regulated cash flows backed by solid investment-grade balance sheets.

Moreover, these sectors have healthy growth pipelines in general, with the likes of Energy Transfer (ET), Enterprise Products Partners (EPD), Enbridge (ENB), Brookfield Infrastructure Partners (BIP), and the aforementioned triple-net lease REITs all having plenty of opportunities for acquisitions and organic growth investments. Last but not least, these sectors could certainly be re-rated higher in terms of their valuations, as their yields stand at relatively high levels compared to their own histories and look particularly attractive relative to where the S&P 500 is trading today.

As a result, these are among my very favorite places to lock in attractive and durable income, along with attractive long-term total return and attractive risk-adjusted total return potential over the long term. Therefore, these are the sorts of stocks I'm loading up on in my 7-8% yielding, low-beta, and high-returning portfolios at High Yield Investor.

data.title: Timken: Priced Already For Stability, Not For Growth
data.description:
The Timken Company (TKR) is a 100+ year old company going through a major internal reset. That, and not any particular surge in demand, has caused a localized upswing in the share price. This is an important distinction in understanding Timken now.

Timken’s revenue grew by 2.7% year over year in Q3. Adjusted EBITDA went up 50 basis points to 17.4%. Free cash flow was $164 million. This was despite a $20+ million tariff loss, and very little volume growth in some segments. That means, earnings did not improve due to demand, but due to cost discipline and pricing - part of that internal reset.

Seeing these results, management has now decided to keep on prioritizing only those business segments that can sustain ~20% EBITDA margins. Other segments will be readjusted. At the same time, they are trying to generate demand through focusing on aftermarkets, cross-sells and geographic expansions.

So the question for investors is no longer whether Timken can defend margins during stress — they can — but whether there’s further upside in earnings, and whether the recent run has already discounted any such upside.

What Timken Does

Timken is a leading manufacturer of engineered bearings and industrial motions components. These are high-end products, not sold on price alone; often, Timken works directly with customers and their engineering teams to design components that can operate under extreme load, heat, and stress. Revenue comes from the products, replacements and service.

The business is split into two segments. The first one is Engineered Bearings. This is the larger and more stable segment. EB makes various types of bearings for multiple applications. These are high-end products designed for long-term reliability, so Timken retains pricing power. Even after tariffs, EB posted positive growth in Q3, with margins near 19%.

The other segment is Industrial Motion. They make gearboxes, couplings, chains, belts, lubrication systems, and linear motion products. These products position next to bearings in the same equipment, but have higher replacement/aftermarket potential. Due to various factors - weak solar demand, solid previous year, delayed spending on maintenance - this segment is seeing lower earnings. However, its margins are actually higher than bearings in normal times.

These two segments have roughly equal sales deriving from distributors/aftermarkets and original equipment sales. Aftermarket replacement demand is less sensitive to short-term OEM dynamics. This is a key reason Timken has been able to expand margins even with modest OEM growth.

Q3 2025 Earnings Improvement

Timken’s earnings mix shift is clear from Q3 numbers. Despite revenue growing 2.7% year over year, organic growth was only 0.6%. That doesn’t sound too exciting, however, what's notable is the strong adjusted EBITDA margins and incremental margins growth I described earlier, despite tariff headwinds. That margin expansion came from three sources.

These three sources are largely directly under management control. First is pricing, which increased across both segments. Second is cost discipline, which reduced material and logistics expenses, particularly in Engineered Bearings. Third is a better mix, with higher margin applications such as wind, aerospace, and general industrial offset weaker areas like solar and agriculture.

Timken’s operating cash flow was a robust $201 million, translating to $164 million of FCF after capex. Net leverage also declined quarter over quarter to 2.1x EBITDA. This helps management continue buybacks, dividends, and targeted reinvestment without straining the balance sheet.

My key point here is that these results did not come through a sudden surge in customer demand. Demand remained soft, with some decline in Industrial Motion, actually. Yet earnings managed to move higher, showing that margins are no longer driven by short-term demand swings. Whatever Timken has done to its internal structure, it has worked.

Current Earnings Mix

Timken’s earnings mix is a little uneven. EB did the heavy lifting in Q3, having sales of $766 million. This was a 3.4% rise year over year, with margins reaching 18.8%. This high margin came through better pricing and operating performance. This was able to offset cost inflation and tariffs even without much volume growth. So we are already close to management’s long-term margin expectations, so EB provides a stable anchor to overall earnings.

IM posted slightly higher EBITDA margin, 19%, but was still lagging on near-term earnings growth. Organic sales went down 3.5% to $391 million, nearly half of EB. This was due to weaker demand from solar and agriculture and lower services revenue. Margins were maintained through pricing and cost control, but earnings were limited by lower volumes. Reported margins may well be similar, but a segment doing $766 million and growing adds far more EBITDA dollars than one doing $391 million but shrinking. This is what makes IM a drag on earnings today, and also offers the potential for upside if volume moves up.

What is important is that IM is not underperforming because it is historically low-margin. Rather, it is underperforming because of low demand from parts of the end-market. Services revenue has also not recovered, and it comes with strong margins. If these things normalize, the IM segment can contribute to incremental earnings without major capital investment.

What Management Is Actually Changing

Considering the recent margin improvement, management, led by a new CEO, is making some important changes. It is using this opportunity to reallocate capital and management focus across the portfolio.

They are using a returns-based approach to reorganize. Like I noted earlier, any business that cannot sustain ~20% EBITDA margin loses management focus, and could be downsized or exited. Those that can are going to be prioritized for investment and expansion. The shift is towards creating a portfolio based on return on capital and earnings contribution.

This shift is clearly seen in Automotives. This segment historically represented around $1 billion of annual revenue, or close to 20% of company sales. Currently, that has been reduced to about 8% of revenue, a reduction of over $600 million. Management has also indicated that around half the remaining automotive business — around 4% of total company revenue — is under active review. Automotive ties up capital but does not offer returns comparable to other segments.

At the same time, management is focusing on specific acquired platforms that already earn acceptable margins but do not have adequate geographic expansion. Within Industrial Motion, this includes Cone Drive (precision gearing for wind and automation), Rollon (linear motion for automation and robotics), and SPINEA (high-precision gearboxes). Each of these businesses serve global customers, but they still make a disproportionate share of revenue from one or two regions. So, for example, Cone Drive is skewed toward North America and Europe, Rollon is mostly Europe, and SPINEA’s revenue base is largely concentrated in Central and Eastern Europe. Management wants to use their current sales force to expand into other geographies, rather than starting new product lines.

A similar approach is happening in specialty bearings. This already generates ~20% EBITDA margin, but, owing to lower footprint rather than demand, earnings are less. Management plans to improve these areas rather than chase volume. The common point is that all these products have sound economics, the focus is now on scale. If executed well, they can support management’s focus of improving through earnings quality over revenue growth.

Timken is also undertaking a $75 million cost-saving program. They have identified overlaps in sales teams, logistics and back-office functions in IM and EB, while IM still operates with a higher cost-to-serve than EB. The focus will be to integrate these to cut costs, with $15 million targeted in Q1 2026 itself.

For investors, what matters is that these actions are internal, and quantifiable. They do not rely on a rebound in the end-market demand. So, instead of top-line growth alone, successful execution will impact segment margin growth, revenue from higher-return segments, and overall sustained FCF.

Because much of this execution is already showing up in margins and cash flow, the focus now turns to valuation and whether the recent move in the stock has already priced in that improvement.

Valuation: What the Numbers Actually Say

At its current share price (~$91), Timken is already being valued as if it can maintain its current profitability, not one where meaningful earnings expansion is still ahead.

Thus, using management’s reaffirmed FY25 adjusted EPS midpoint of $5.25, the stock trades at around 17-18x forward earnings. This is close to Timken’s own five-year average, and in line with economic peers. From an enterprise perspective, Timken trades at around 10-11x forward EBITDA, based on trailing adjusted EBITDA just under $800 million and net debt of roughly $1.7 billion. Again, that multiple is consistent with a business expected to sustain margins in the high-teens, not one that will expand beyond that level.

The free cash flow situation is solid, but already accounted for. Management expects ~$375 million of FCF in 2025, which implies an FCF yield in the mid-single digits. This will comfortably support buybacks and dividends, but does not, by itself, indicate multiple expansion. For that to happen, FCF must grow faster than earnings in this situation - which is not yet happening.

If margins do move closer to management’s target 20% EBITDA across much of the portfolio, current valuation may start appearing conservative. So, a 200-250 basis point margin lift on Timken’s current revenue base would translate to around $90-120 million of incremental EBITDA, or about $1.00-$1.30 per share. This would be an earnings change significant enough to justify higher multiples.

The problem, however, is timing and current visibility. Today’s valuation already assumes that a) margins will remain near 17–18%, b) cost savings as planned are already realized or to be delivered, c) pricing already offsets tariff, and d) IM stabilizes, not deteriorates.

What the current valuation does not assume is that IM becomes a net contributor to earnings growth, or that management’s planned portfolio reshuffle definitely lifts consolidated margins above current levels. Until one of these shows up clearly in reported numbers, the current valuation is tied to the current earnings base.

Risks And Limitations

So we see that the investment case rests on execution, not on-demand improvement. That narrows the risk set, but does not eliminate it.

Risk one is that IM may remain sluggish for longer than accounted for. Much of the potential upside depends on services revenue and maintenance activity improving. These are higher-margin segments. But if demand remains low, IM may still stabilize margins but not be able to contribute to earnings.

A second risk is that portfolio reshaping may be difficult to execute without hurting earnings. It may not be easy to exit automotive, scale acquired platforms geographically, even if necessary. In that case, earnings improvements may be delayed.

The third risk is that the current valuation leaves little margin for errors. Current price already assumes durable margins and steady cash flow. However, these things remaining just stable won’t produce upside. For upsides, they need to grow — and if they don’t, the bull case will falter.

Conclusion: Conditional Buy

Timken has already shown it can maintain stable margins during bad times. However, the stock is not cheap. Valuation has already accounted for stable margins. There are no aggressive assumptions, so this gives investors a dependable fallback option.

Upside, though, depends on improving margins that will produce higher earnings. IM also needs to contribute to growth, while EB should at least keep steady. Recent history does show this may be possible with a bit of execution. If investors believe such an execution is possible, TKR is a conditional buy.

data.title: Boeing Is Winning With The 737 MAX
data.description:
Boeing (BA) stock has surged 14.8% since my last report, outperforming the S&P 500’s 1.6% gain. The U.S. jet maker finally seems to be on the right track, increasing its production with quality and safety as a cornerstone, and that also results in substantial order inflow from customers. Each order can be considered as a token of confidence from customers that Boeing is on the right track. One of those orders comes from Alaska Airlines (ALK), and in this report, I discuss why that order is an extremely important one for Boeing beyond the value of the deal.

Alaska Airlines Books Largest Ever Order With Boeing

On Jan. 7, Boeing announced that Alaska Airlines signed an agreement to purchase a total of 145 airplanes, including options. The deal consists of five Boeing 787 airplanes on firm order with no specification on which model will be ordered and 105 Boeing 737 MAX 10 airplanes on firm order with options for another 35 airplanes. The firm part of the order is worth around $6.6 billion at market value, with another $2 billion tied to the options for a total of $8.6 billion. By the end of November, Alaska Airlines had 71 unfilled orders for the Boeing 737 MAX and 6 unfilled orders for the Boeing 787. The addition of the 105 orders would lift Alaska Airlines’ order book to around 175 airplanes. The order is particularly meaningful as it comes almost exactly two years after a Boeing 737 MAX 9 operating for Alaska Airlines suffered rapid decompression when a plugged exit door blew out.

That incident set a chain of events in motion that led to Boeing’s production rate being capped at 38 airplanes a month, a cap that was lifted towards the end of 2025. Furthermore, in the aftermath of the incident, David Calhoun was replaced as CEO of Boeing, and Boeing acquired Spirit AeroSystems to better control quality. So, the purchase agreement is an important one as it comes from a customer whose order indicates a strong sign of confidence in Boeing’s product and management.

Additionally, the Boeing 737 MAX part of the order is for the yet-to-be-certified Boeing 737 MAX 10. As a result, the order can also be seen as a sign of confidence in the certification process and timeline.

A View On The Airplane Program Backlog And Production Rate

By the end of November, Boeing had 6,616 airplanes in backlog. Over 70% of the backlog is for the Boeing 737 MAX, and around 16% is for the Boeing 787. So, with this purchase, Alaska Airlines is buying the two most popular Boeing jets. The acquisition of five Boeing 787s is not going to do much for the program, adding less than half a percent to the backlog, excluding possible mutations in December from deliveries and cancellations. For the Boeing 737 MAX program, the order would add 2.2% to the backlog, excluding mutations. More importantly, for the Boeing 737 MAX 10, it adds 8.1% to the backlog, and Alaska Airlines is joining Delta Air Lines and United Airlines as customers for the largest Boeing 737 MAX 10 model.

Boeing has been granted permission to increase production for the Boeing 737 MAX to 42 airplanes per month, and the company is expected to increase production every six months by increments of five airplanes.

Using the rule of thumb that a healthy narrow-body backlog covers five years of production, prior to the announcement the supported production rate for the Boeing 737 MAX was almost 80 airplanes per month, and after the announcement this would increase to 81.3 airplanes per month, excluding new December orders from other customers and December airplane deliveries and cancellations.

Boeing is ramping up production, and eventually, the U.S. jet maker will open a fourth line in Everett, which would be exclusive to the MAX 10 model. Each line supports a production level of 21 airplanes per month, bringing the total to 84 airplanes per month. Including the order, Boeing is essentially 97% sold out in the years ahead at this rate, up from 94%. Specifically to the Boeing 737 MAX 10, the supported rate would be 23.3 airplanes per month, which surpasses the output of the fourth line, and that allows Boeing a lot of flexibility to navigate any changes in customer fleet planning.

So, Boeing is currently ramping production towards a production level of 84 airplanes per month in what should be a multi-year path, and at that rate the company is almost sold out.

Certification Efforts For The Boeing 737 MAX 10

Following the Boeing 737 MAX crisis, certification of the Boeing 777X, MAX 7, and MAX 10 has suffered several delays. Certification of the 777X moved into the next phase in November 2025, with certification now having slipped to 2027 from 2026. The MAX 7 and MAX 10 models are set to be certified this year. The most recent news is that the MAX 10 entered the second phase in the certification process days ago, which is another highlight for the MAX 10 this year and brings certification one step closer, as well as the financial benefits that the MAX 10 will eventually bring for Boeing. With each advancement in the certification process, the risk of additional delays reduces, so this is definitely good news for Boeing.

Conclusion: Boeing Has Commercial And Certification Momentum With The MAX 10

While order numbers for December have yet to be published at the time of writing, it can already be said that Boeing has had a strong year in terms of order inflow for its key commercial airplane programs, and that signals confidence from customers in the production discipline and certification process. With the Alaska Airlines order, that momentum seems to be carrying on in 2026, and we also see that the MAX 10 is getting a step closer to commercial service entry and eventually driving up Boeing’s financial performance.

While Boeing still has some key hurdles to take and clean up its balance sheet, we are seeing extremely strong signs that Boeing is on the right track, and the order from Alaska Airlines for the Boeing 737 MAX puts on display confidence from a key Boeing customer in the certification timeline and approach towards safety and quality in the production system. Currently, I remain bullish on Boeing’s long-term prospects to drive value for shareholders through disciplined production with safety and quality as a cornerstone and rising free cash flow as a welcome result.

data.title: PEPS: Generating Alpha (Usually)
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman

Parametric Equity Plus ETF (PEPS) came across my radar when I was recently covering a sister fund, the Parametric Equity Premium Income ETF (PAPI). I have a couple of issues with PAPI's strategy and construction that make it avoidable; however, for those interested in that fund, they can read my previous write-up. PEPS was an interesting fund because it is designed to generate alpha against the S&P 500 Index. While that is a goal of a lot of active managers, this fund has actually achieved that—at least since its inception and during most market scenarios.

This is not an income-focused investment such as like PAPI with a ~7.5% distribution rate that pays monthly. Instead, PEPS has a modest trailing twelve-month ~1% distribution rate that is paid quarterly. On the other hand, given that the construction of the underlying holdings is similar to the S&P 500 Index, we should see dividends increase over time. Therefore, PEPS could see similar dividend growth over the long term. That's why, while the fund is more appropriate for a long-term total return/growth investor, some dividend growth investors may find some appeal in an allocation as well. That could be even if it's a smaller allocation.

PEPS Basics

  • Dividend Frequency: Quarterly
  • Dividend Yield: 0.99% TTM Distribution Yield
  • Expense Ratio: 0.29%
  • Leverage: N/A
  • Managed Assets: $25.25 million
  • Structure: Active ETF

PEPS' investment objective is to "provide access to a more consistent source of alpha than traditional stock selection, that is less dependent on market environment by employing a rules-based derivatives strategy." The fund also further "utilizes active tax management techniques that aim to produce a tax-efficient enhanced equity exposure."

PEPS comes with a fairly low expense ratio for an actively managed fund. The average ETF comes in with an expense ratio of around 50 basis points, and active funds are often even higher than that.

Despite generating alpha, which we'll discuss further below, the fund has not garnered much in terms of total managed assets in just over a year now. The fund's inception is November 7, 2024. Fortunately, the underlying portfolio holdings are all S&P 500 Index components, which brings plenty of liquidity for even the largest investors.

Portfolio Strategy and Construction

In the construction of the fund, they take a more sample-based approach of investing in fewer than the ~500 holdings the S&P 500 Index carries. Instead, they carry around 200 holdings, but several of these are option positions. The largest holdings are reflective of the index, complete with its heavier concentration in the Magnificent 7 names.

This isn't that unusual for S&P 500 Index tracking funds at all, to carry fewer holdings. As long as they have heavier allocations to the names that truly matter, they will track the index pretty closely.

This is then reflected in the overall sector allocation of the fund as well, which also reflects an S&P 500 Index overweight allocation to the technology sector.

With that said, where the fund differentiates itself is through incorporating various options strategies—which is where it is able to generate alpha relative to the index.

One of the strategies is to write index calls at a notional value of the entire underlying portfolio. In a traditional covered call strategy alone, a fund will be exposed to an upside cap, but that isn't the case for PEPS because they also combine the fund with a "beta-neutral" strategy, whereby they also buy long calls.

The Fund is an actively managed exchange-traded fund (“ETF”). The investment objective of the Fund is to seek to provide long-term capital appreciation. The Adviser and Parametric Portfolio Associates LLC (the “Sub-Adviser”) seek to fulfill the Fund’s objective by (1) investing in an underlying base portfolio of equity securities (the “Equity Portfolio”) that primarily include equity securities of companies included in the Solactive GBS United States 500 Index (the “Equity Portfolio Index”) and (2) generating incremental total return via “beta-neutral” call overwriting that combines selling call options on the S&P 500® Index (the “Underlying Index”) with offsetting long equity exposure, including through investment in futures on the Underlying Index or in options on the Underlying Index or on the SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (the “Underlying ETF”), including Flexible Exchange Options (“FLEX Options”) that reference the Underlying Index or Underlying ETF.

With this, the beta moves back to 1 or very close to it, meaning it should move very similarly to the overall market. However, the options strategy isn't done by just going long a call option to gain greater market exposure; they create a synthetic long position via options from long call and short put positions. The long call and short put options position carries the same expiration date and the same number of contracts to create a synthetic long exposure.

That isn't all the options contracts they utilize, either; they are also long put contracts. By buying puts, which can offer some downside protection, and while simultaneously writing call contracts, you actually create a sort of collar strategy. A collar strategy is often considered very defensive, and those funds often underperform the S&P 500 Index across all scenarios except in sharply lower markets.

In this case, the fund does not use a standard collar where they enter into the long put and short call positions with the same expiration; instead, they take a laddering approach.

I know this is all fairly complex relative to the option strategies I generally share at Cash Builder Opportunities, which are overwhelmingly limited to short call and put contracts with sometimes buy-writes, short strangles and credit spreads involving a couple of different moves.

So with that, below is a better illustration of taking a look exactly at the options contracts that PEPS will be holding. Below is how the fund was positioned at the end of September 30, 2025, its last fiscal year-end reporting (some of the contracts have expired at this point, but for illustration purposes, it still helps to provide context):

By writing short-dated calls with a laddered approach, they can more effectively control the upside cap that these contracts could run into as well. This is because the likelihood of significant moves is lower, generally resulting in the expectation that most of these contracts expire worthless or get rolled at a net premium profit.

The Results - Generating Alpha In Most Periods

Since the fund's inception, albeit which isn't all that long of a track record so far, the fund has generated alpha relative to the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY).

For those interested in other timeframes that the fund provides a table for, they have also generated alpha in each of the standard time frames except for the 1-month period.

However, one might be more interested in the specific period of the April tariff market sell-off—which is really the only real sell-off we've seen with a substantial drop since the fund's inception. Unfortunately, it was a very short-lived sell-off as it started on April 2, with the tariff announcements. It then bottomed on April 8, causing the market rebound that unfolded.

In that scenario, PEPS did underperform relative to SPY because the downturn wasn't sharp enough for the long puts to really kick in. The puts they write are pretty deep out-of-the-money, which means they're more like catastrophic insurance. Since they are also laddered, only the closer-dated contracts would have likely seen a meaningful lift.

Further, this would have meant rolling their short calls more expensive and offset some of the gains the put contracts saw as well. In general, had we moved to a bear market or prolonged downturn, then we may potentially see alpha during that scenario as well.

For another Parametric fund for investors looking for that downside protection, they have the Parametric Hedged Equity ETF (PHEQ). This fund utilizes a put spread collar strategy. This is a defensive strategy, but it is actually less defensive than a traditional collar strategy as well if the market drops too far. In that same period we saw above, PHEQ was able to mitigate some of the downside.

With that said, back to PEPS, as PHEQ could be a discussion for another day. Given that the PEPS also writes options, they are collecting premiums, which means in a flat market, they can still generate some positive returns even if SPY is flat. However, a truly flat market tends to be fairly rare for extended periods of time.

One last measurement period we want to take a look at is the recovery of the market, starting from April 9 to the time of writing this. We know that in a standard call writing fund, the fund will underperform as the underlying rises rapidly and breaches the short call strike prices selected. However, given the synthetic long strategy to shift PEPS back to a beta of 1, that shouldn't occur.

Indeed, when looking at that performance since then, we can see that the fund did not underperform and was able to generate alpha under that scenario as well—as we would have expected.

Conclusion

The Parametric team created PEPS to seek alpha, and that alpha has been achieved during most time frames. This should generally occur during flat or rising markets, even sharply rising markets. However, if declines come too rapidly and are relatively short-lived, we should typically see PEPS underperform. We saw that play out in the April sell-off. This is a fund that would be appropriate for a total return/growth investor, and potentially, some dividend growth investors may find it appealing as well.

data.title: Forgent Power Solutions Begins IPO Effort As AI Data Center Power Demand Jumps
data.description:
Forgent Is Growing Via M&A Activity

Forgent Power Solutions, Inc. (FPS) has filed to raise capital in an IPO of its Class A common stock, according to SEC S-1 registration information.

FPS manufactures and sells electrical distribution equipment for various industries in North America.

Growth has been strong from M&A deals and the company has been investing in multiplying its manufacturing capacity as it seeks to expand further into prefabricated systems.

I’ll provide a final opinion when we learn what management’s pricing and valuation expectations are.

What Does Forgent Do?

Dayton, Minnesota-based Forgent Power Solutions, Inc. was founded to develop a wide range of electric power distribution customized products for digital and industrial customers.

Management is led by Chief Executive Officer Mr. Gary J. Niederpruem, who has been with the firm since May 2025 and was previously COO at Sasser Family Companies in the transportation asset management industry and CEO of Cenergistic, an energy conservation and sustainability company.

The company's primary offerings include:

  • Transformers

  • Switchgear & Panels

  • Prefabricated Solutions

  • Transfer Switches & Connection Systems.

The firm's revenue contribution by product segment and market is shown in the pie charts below:

Forgent has booked fair market value investment of $588 million through September 30, 2025, from investors, including private equity firm Neos Partners.

Selling, G&A expenses as a percentage of total revenue have fallen as revenues have increased, as the figures below indicate:

Selling, G&A

Expenses vs. Revenue

Period

Percentage

Three Months Ended Sept. 30, 2025

18.9%

FYE June 30, 2025

19.4%

FYE June 30, 2024

25.8%

(Source - SEC.)

The Selling, G&A efficiency multiple, i.e., how many dollars of additional revenue are produced from each dollar of Selling, G&A expense, fell to 2.4x in the most recent reporting period, as shown in the table below:

Selling, G&A

Efficiency Rate

Period

Multiple

Three Months Ended Sept. 30, 2025

2.4

FYE June 30, 2025

3.5

(Source - SEC.)

What Is Forgent’s Market?

According to a 2026 market research report by Mordor Intelligence, the global market for electric power transmission and distribution equipment was an estimated $545 billion in 2026 and was forecast to exceed $755 billion by 2031.

This represents a projected CAGR of 6.74% from 2026 to 2031.

The primary factors driving this expected growth are strong mandates to increase electric network buildouts and grid modernization efforts in North America and Europe.

Also, potential constraints on growth include price volatility for electrical-grade copper and steel, permitting delays and skilled labor shortages for high voltage field services.

Major competitive vendors or industry players include the following firms:

  • Vertiv (VRT)

  • PCX Corporation - Hubbell (HUBB)

  • Schneider Electric SE (SBGSF)

  • IEM Holdings Group

  • Hitachi Energy (HTHIY)

  • GE-Prolec Transformers (GE)

  • Systems Control - Hubbell (HUBB)

  • Cooper Power

  • nVent Electric plc (NVT)

  • Eaton Corporation plc (ETN)

  • WEG S.A. (WEGZY)

  • Others.

The electrical distribution equipment industry is enjoying strong demand tailwinds from AI and cloud data center expansion, grid resiliency investments, a reshoring of manufacturing capacity and customer preferences shifting to pre-assembled solutions.

The firm bids on and sells to projects in the data center, grid and industrial energy markets, primarily in North America.

Forgent highlighted its "diversity" in markets, product categories and customers, as shown here:

Below is a thumbnail SWOT analysis for Forgent:

Strengths

  • Broad product portfolio, vertically integrated manufacturing, market presence in diverse markets.

Weaknesses

  • High operating complexity, manufacturing concentration in N. America, reliance on engineered-to-order products.

Opportunities

  • Fast growth of data centers from the generative AI demand explosion, grid modernization efforts, increased demand for prefabricated and integrated power solutions.

Threats

  • Supply chain disruptions, increasingly intense competition from established OEMs, technological shift risks, changes in trade policy or tariffs.

Forgent’s Recent Financial Results

The firm’s recent financial performance is summarized as shown below:

  • High top line revenue growth from M&A.

  • Growing gross profit but reduced gross margin.

  • Increasing operating profit and operating margin.

  • Variable cash flow from operations.

Below are the major financial results from the firm’s current registration statement:

Total Revenue

(Source - SEC.)

The firm’s operating performance as a percentage of revenue is shown in the chart below:

The chart indicates that Gross Margin has fluctuated within a range, while Selling, G&A expenses as a percentage of revenue have trended lower over time.

Forgent’s IPO Plan

FPS intends to sell shares of Class A common stock in an IPO likely to be substantially higher than its placeholder $100 million figure.

No existing or possibly new shareholders have indicated an interest in acquiring additional shares of the IPO, at least as of the first IPO prospectus.

Class A stockholders will receive one vote per share.

Class B shareholders will also have one vote per share, but will not be entitled to any distributions or dividends.

The Continuing Equity Owners (from the various underlying LLC operating companies and controlled by Neos) will have voting control of the company immediately post-IPO.

Company, shareholder and executive lock-ups will be for the typical 180 days after the effective date of the IPO prospectus.

According to Forgent’s most recent regulatory filing, it plans to use the net proceeds as follows:

We intend to use the net proceeds we receive from this offering to indirectly purchase [an as yet undisclosed amount of] Opco LLC Interests ...or Opco LLC Interests if the underwriters exercise in full their option to purchase additional shares of Class A common stock) from Opco at a price per unit equal to the initial public offering price per share of Class A common stock in this offering less the underwriting discounts and commissions.

Opco intends to use the approximately $ [undisclosed amount] in net proceeds it receives from the sale of Opco LLC Interests to us to redeem Opco LLC Interests from the Existing Opco LLC Owners at a price per unit equal to the initial public offering price per share of Class A common stock in this offering less the underwriting discounts and commissions and any withholding taxes.

(Source - SEC.)

The firm’s presentation for its roadshow is not yet available.

Pertaining to potential legal exposure, leadership said there are currently no legal claims that would have a material adverse effect on its business or financial condition.

Listed underwriters of the IPO are Goldman Sachs, Jefferies, Morgan Stanley, J.P. Morgan, BofA Securities, Barclays, TD Cowen, MUFG, Wolfe | Nomura Alliance, KeyBanc Capital Markets and Oppenheimer & Co.

Capital And Liquidity Dynamics

As of September 30, 2025, Forgent had $81.4 million in cash and equivalents and $993.4 million in total liabilities, of which $501 million was debt.

The company has generated free cash flow for the twelve months ended September 30, 2025, of negative ($72 million) on $106 million of capital expenditures.

FPS said it is coming to the conclusion of a multi-year $205 million initiative to grow its manufacturing capacity to be able to support up to $5 billion in total revenues.

So, with a lot of moving parts, from its significant M&A activity to its subsequent capital expenditure-intensive period, determining shareholder friendliness is difficult.

The firm’s net debt to EBITDA multiple is 2.9x, which is higher than public peers, but once the heavy CapEx is over, the free cash flow should return to positive and make this less of a worry.

So, the company is essentially still in "build" mode, so capital allocation looks less than ideal, although that should change significantly starting with 2026’s financial results.

Forgent Is Growing through M&A and Capacity Expansion

Fogent is seeking to go public in another private equity firm IPO early in 2026.

FPS’s financials have generated top line revenue growth from M&A activity, increasing gross profit but decreasing gross margin, higher operating profit and operating margin and fluctuating cash flow from operations.

Selling, G&A expenses as a percentage of total revenue have fallen as revenue has grown, and its Selling, G&A efficiency multiple fell to 2.4x in the most recent reporting period.

The firm currently plans to pay no cash dividends to holders of Class A shares.

The company’s recent capital spending history shows it has spent heavily on capital expenditures as part of its plan to substantially expand its manufacturing capacity by the end of calendar 2026.

As to unit economics, Forgent’s primary focus has been on selling custom solutions, which generally produce higher gross margins than standardized products.

However, the firm’s growth strategy includes continuing to expand its manufacturing capacity to capture market share from capacity-limited competitors, expanding its prefab solutions, and introducing new products focused on the higher power density/data center markets to take advantage of the AI infrastructure buildout.

So, this growth strategy may result in lower overall gross margins going forward, but higher market share and gross profit.

Backlog looks quite strong, at just over $1 billion as of September 30, 2025, up 44% YoY and a backlog-to-revenue coverage of 1.36x.

The market opportunity for providing electrical distribution equipment in North America is large and is expected to grow significantly in the coming years, so the firm enjoys positive growth dynamics in its primary market.

The company has manufacturing concentration risks in North America as well as customer concentration risks in one region.

It also has supplier concentration risks for certain specialized components and materials.

It is designated as an ‘emerging growth company’, which enables management to produce less information for shareholders.

With a current revenue run rate of above $1 billion and increasing capacity along with plans to expand further into prefabricated systems, Forgent looks well-positioned to take advantage of the strong growth outlook for North American data center and grid modernization growth in the coming years.

There will likely be significant institutional investor demand for the IPO, assuming a reasonable valuation.

I’ll provide an update when we learn more about the IPO from management.

Expected IPO Pricing Date: To be announced.

data.title: Enterprise Products Partners: Steady 7% Yield Amid A Volatile Energy Backdrop
data.description:
It has been a tough last 12 months for owners of high-yield energy midstream partnerships. Key ETFs tracking the space have produced mid-single-digit total returns since mid-January 2025, all while global equities have enjoyed a torrid rally since April. The S&P 500 ETF (SPY) has returned 21% YoY, while ex-U.S. shares are up almost 40%, dividends included.

Today, I’m revisiting Enterprise Products Partners L.P. Common Units (EPD). I was bullish on the units back in October 2024. Since then, the return has been impressive—up more than 20%, actually outperforming SPY by a modest margin. Today, I reiterate a "Buy" rating. I’ll offer a refreshed valuation and an updated look at the technicals.

MLPs Lagging the Global Equity Market YoY, Weak Energy Sector In General

Back in October, EPD reported a mixed set of quarterly results. Q3 GAAP EPS of $0.61 missed the Wall Street consensus forecast of $0.68, while revenue of $12.0 billion, down almost 13% from the same period a year earlier, was a material $220 million beat. Its adjusted cash flow from operations (CFFO) summed to $2.1 billion over the July through September stretch, which was about unchanged from 2024.

Units actually traded modestly higher after the mixed report, rising 1.5%--the best reaction to earnings going back at least three years, according to data from Option Research & Technology Services. Looking ahead to the Feb. 3 Q4 report, the options market prices in a low 2.7% share price swing based on the at-the-money straddle expiring soonest after the release. Units of the now $69 billion energy sector company feature a low 15% implied volatility, while short interest on EPD is modest at 1.34%.

Looking back on the quarter that was, the $2.4 billion of adjusted EBITDA was a bit shy of expectations, and the management team was less upbeat compared to after the Q2 update. What’s more, executives were somewhat cautious on the future growth outlook, perhaps due to delays in commissioning projects like the Bahia pipeline. The good news was that the partnership increased its buyback program to $5 billion, a sort of bullish body language move that was perhaps sparked by the post-earnings rally.

On the call, EPD expressed optimism that it would return to target return levels by the end of this year, helped by new project EBITDA contribution, while a steady capex forecast should help ensure strong free cash flow generation. Still, volatility in the oil and gas market (as we have seen in the past few months) was cited as a possible headwind.

For unitholders, the partnership continued its disciplined capital return strategy in Q3 2025, distributing $0.545 per unit (up 3.8% YoY) and repurchasing $80 million of common units. The company also maintained its strong financial health with a 3.3x leverage ratio (targeting about 3.0x), $3.6 billion in liquidity, and an adjusted CFFO payout ratio of 58%. EPD’s total gross operating margin was $2.39 billion in Q3 2025, down modestly from $2.45 billion in Q3 2024 and $2.48 billion sequentially.

EPD hiked its dividend on Thursday, Jan. 9, 2026, while before that, the firm announced a partnership with Exxon Mobil (XOM), forming a joint venture to expand EPD’s Bahia pipeline.

On the earnings outlook, EPS growth is expected to verify negative for CY 2025, but then inflect positive for 2026 and through the out year. Revenue growth could also turn positive, but weakness in the oil and gas market could result in further earnings downgrades.

Indeed, there have been a high 10 bottom-line negative revisions in the past 90 days compared to just a lone upgrade. The good news is that EPD is highly profitable, now with $1.43 of free cash flow per share over the past 12 months.

EPD: Revenue & Earnings Forecasts, Mixed EPS Revision Trends

On valuation, I have key updates to my Q4 2024 analysis. If we assume normalized per-unit earnings of $2.85 (slightly below the current consensus, given recent softer trends in EPD’s EBITDA revisions) and apply a 12x forward multiple, then units should trade near $34.

That multiple is appropriate if long-term earnings growth steadies in the mid-high single digits. Bear in mind that unitholders are paid to wait, now that the yield is 6.9%.

EPD: Near the Long-Term Average P/E, High Yield

Key risks for EPD include rising competition across domestic basins, namely the Permian, while regional operational risks were particularly called out by analysts on the Q3 earnings call.

And while the leverage situation looks fine to me, the street has grown mildly worried about the capex allocation strategy regarding free cash flow for buybacks versus debt repayment. Of course, lower oil and gas prices (and domestic demand) could weigh on the top line in the quarters ahead.

Competitor Analysis

Looking ahead, corporate event data provided by Wall Street Horizon show a confirmed Q4 2025 earnings date of Tuesday, Feb. 3, BMO. Before that, units pay a $0.55 distribution on Friday, Jan. 30.

Corporate Event Risk Calendar

The Technical Take

While units remain a solid value in my estimate, EPD’s technical situation is not ideal. Notice in the chart below that it has been a sideways and choppy trade for the better part of the last year. Resistance is apparent at the $33 mark, while support is close to $30. What’s more, the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph has ranged in a neutral zone from 30 to 70.

Also take a look at the long-term 200-day moving average. It’s now falling, suggesting that the bears control the primary trend. The upside there is that a bullish golden cross pattern played out just recently, when the 50dma climbed above the 200dma. Units are also above both moving averages—a good sign. Long-term resistance is near $34.50, while an uptrend support line comes into the scene at $29.

EPD: Neutral Trading Range, Falling 200dma

The Bottom Line

I have a "Buy" rating on EPD. I see units as undervalued enough to remain bullish on the risk/reward profile, while the near-$7 yield is quite attractive as Treasury yields potentially venture lower as the year progresses.

data.title: Flutter Entertainment: Prediction Market Is Lucrative, But Competition Is Fierce
data.description:
Introduction

Recently, Flutter Entertainment plc (FLUT) said its share price went back to the $200s, so I thought I’d go through its performance over the last while and give some comments on what the best opportunity for it is going forward. Prediction markets look promising, but the management’s plan of rolling it out slowly may not be proactive enough to beat out the competition.

Briefly On Performance

Starting from the top, we can see that the company’s revenues over the last two years have been steadily growing. On average, the company saw its revenue increase by around 13% a quarter, which is rather respectable. The most recent quarter, Q3 ’25, showed growth accelerating to 17% y/y, which slightly beat estimates by around $13m. The international revenue segment saw fantastic growth of around 21%, while the U.S. showed around 9%. iGaming revenue in the U.S., however, grew a whopping 44% y/y. Casino operations are doing phenomenally well.

Over the same timeframe, I am a little disappointed to see that the company’s margins across the board saw very little change. It seems that the company is betting heavily on the U.S. markets and is investing a lot of money in growth, like FanDuel Predicts and other M&As. I hope that it will work out in the end.

It also seems that on a GAAP basis, the company is still not profitable. This past quarter, the company was particularly deep in the red according to the GAAP reporting, as its GAAP EPS came in at a loss of $3.91 a share due to a massive goodwill impairment charge related to the ban of online real money gaming in India. The company wrote off all of the operations there.

It’s not a massive hit to the company, as Junglee was expected to bring around $200m full-year 2025 revenue, or around 1% of total revenue.

Additionally, it seems like the management isn’t doing a particularly good job of utilizing the company’s assets and shareholder capital. Since the company’s net income (GAAP) is negative, so are ROA and ROE. The company’s ROTC, which I like to look at to see if the company has any pricing power and a moat, is very low, too. Well below 10%, I like to see at a minimum. What’s worse is that all these metrics began to decline at the beginning of 2025.

Let’s take a look at the company’s financial position. As of Q3 ’25, the company had around $1.7B in cash and equivalents, against $12B in long-term debt. The company secured decent covenants on the massive debt pile, needing to have a net debt to EBITDA ratio of less than 5.2x, and right now, it’s at around 4x, with S&P Global (SPGI) rating its debt as low risk at Bbb3. Nothing matures until 2028, so there is also a lot of time to come up with the capital necessary. Operating cash flow for the nine months ended September 30th was down to $756m from $950m, but that is still enough to cover any interest expenses it may have, as well as its decent cash pile. I don’t see any liquidity or solvency risks.

Overall, I don’t see anything exciting regarding the company’s performance over the last couple of years. It has been struggling to turn a GAAP profit as its operating expenses continue to outweigh the revenue growth. However, I hope that is going to change in the next while, otherwise, the company will remain unattractive to me.

Predictions Markets Boom

Prediction markets essentially came out of nowhere, and apparently, it is not called betting. If it were betting, many states, including the biggest ones in the U.S. will not allow it to be a thing. So, what’s the difference? Predictions markets seem to be very similar to a derivatives contract you may find with your favorite company. Let’s take an example of an options contract.

Let’s look at a call example. If you think a company’s share is going to be over $50 in a month, then you purchase a call; you say, “Yes, I believe that to be true.” Another person might not be this optimistic and can sell you this contract, saying, “No, I don’t think so.” That is essentially what prediction markets are. There are scenarios based on whatever is available in the world, and people can make predictions on whether that event will happen or not. The structure differs in a way that in gambling, there are fixed-odds against the house, or bookies, whereas prediction markets are peer-to-peer and are regulated by the CME (CME), which acts as the Designated Contract Market and a clearing house for FanDuel Predicts, which is Flutter’s new revenue segment that seems to be very promising.

Polymarket and Kalshi are currently dominating this space, but many players are beginning to enter the space, like Robinhood (HOOD), DraftKings (DKNG), and Flutter. These companies already have a massive customer base, so it is not going to be as hard to get into the market, especially since prediction markets are not seen as gambling, which means this can be made available nationally in all 50 states. Flutter is taking it slow, by first offering it in 5 states so far. The company is testing the waters in Alabama, Alaska, North Carolina, North Dakota, and South Dakota.

These states, as far as I know, do not allow online sports betting, so it seems that the company is taking a very cautious step forward. It is a lot slower than what its rival, DKNG, has done recently. It went ahead and offered prediction markets to 38 states at the end of December, which are a mix of non-betting states (17 states) and betting states. The non-betting states include Texas and California, which are massive.

This seems to be the only exciting opportunity for the company right now. With the Nevada Gaming Control Board enforcing bans on unlicensed sports predictions in 2025, companies like FLUT and DKNG withdrew sportsbook apps to launch Predictions instead. So, we can see where these companies see the future heading. I think they are going about it a bit too slowly. DKNG is taking aggressive steps to cement itself as one of the dominant players in the space, so it will be interesting to see how this plan is going to turn out.

Right now, all the focus of predictions market is on the U.S. region, but I believe having a presence in the UK and Ireland through subsidiaries like Sky Bet, Paddy Power, and Betfair could potentially unlock a much higher TAM opportunity. No plans as of yet, of course, but it’s something worth considering, especially if the full launch goes well in the U.S.

Closing Comments

Right now, I’m not itching to spend any money on Flutter Entertainment’s hope of successfully launching the prediction market app. The big players like Polymarket and Kalshi have a massive lead here, and the websites offer so many more opportunities compared to what DraftKings or FanDuel offers right now that it is hard to recommend buying any of these two right now, just on the hopes that there is enough room for all to exist.

DraftKings is being a bit more proactive when it comes to this opportunity. Both of these companies just launched the product, so I will be interested to see what kind of numbers they will do in the next quarter’s report. Is it the company’s ticket to consistent profitability in the future? We’ll have to wait and see. FLUT’s shares are a lot more attractive after such a massive dip recently, but I need to see the potential of prediction markets before I commit.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: ZIM Integrated: Takeover Uncertainties Met With Muted Volatility - Buy Call Options
data.description:
ZIM stock: takeover developments

My last analysis on ZIM Integrated Shipping Services Ltd. (NYSE:ZIM) was published on Nov 25 under a title of “ZIM Integrated Q3: Let Your Profit Run.” The article rated the stock as a Buy and other basic information of the article was shown in the screenshot below. Since then, there have been a few material developments surrounding this Israeli shipping company. In the rest of this follow-up article, I will concentrate on the top 2 on my list: the ongoing takeover discussions and the change of its IV (implied volatility) in the option market. My thesis is that the moderated IV underestimates the true uncertainties, thus providing a favorable setup for a long call option strategy.

Shortly since my last writing, ZIM announced that it received a take private offer from company CEO and President Eli Glickman and Rami Unger. Shortly after that, ZIM board also announced the receival of other takeover offers/interests (see more details of these developments quoted below). To further compound the uncertainty, the Israeli Companies Authority also entered the picture as "the state has the right to oppose" a potential sale of more than 24% of ZIM's shares.

ZIM press release (Dec 22, 2025): As part of the review of strategic alternatives that has been on-going during the past several months, the ZIM Board of Directors received competitive proposals from multiple strategic parties to acquire all the outstanding ordinary shares of the Company, and it is currently evaluating such proposals with a focus on delivering significant value to all the Company's shareholders. The ZIM Board of Directors has also received a revised proposal from an entity owned by Eli Glickman, the Company's Chief Executive Officer and President, and Rami Ungar. After careful consideration, the ZIM Board of Directors concluded that this proposal significantly undervalued the Company and informed the management-led entity that its revised proposal was declined.

ZIM stock: implied volatility in the option market

I expect these above uncertainties to heighten ZIM’s share price fluctuations in the near future, which have already been quite wild historically due to the highly cyclical nature of its earnings to begin with (more on this in a minute). Yet, the stock’s IV is quite muted in the option market.

To wit, the following data were taken from MarketChameleon and present the current IV of ZIM together with some historical context. As seen, the current IV is only 51.5, lower than the 52-week average of 56.9 by about 10%. It is also about 12% below its 20-day moving average of 58.4 and about 10% below its 20-day HV (historical volatility of 56.9). Expanding the horizon wider, the current IV is also about 12% below the 252-day HV of 58.4).

Next, I will explain why the combination of muted IV and the rue underlying uncertainties provides a favorable setup to go long on its call options.

ZIM stock: options are attractively priced

To better contextualize the details of the call options, let’s take quick digression and look at ZIM’s recent and income earnings. As seen in the snapshot below, ZIM released its last earnings report (ER) on Nov 20, 2025, and the upcoming ER is scheduled for Mar 12, 2026. In the last quarter, ZIM earned an EPS Of $1.03 on non-GAAP basis. In contrast, analysts’ latest EPS revisions (with 2 downward revisions and no upward revisions) point to a loss of $0.99 per share. Such extreme earnings swing is not only limited to the next quarter, but is observed quite often as seen in the next chart. As seen, in the past 5 quarters, the company’s EPS varied from a peak of $9.34 to $0.19, and its sales exhibited YOY changes from +117% to -35%.

Such large volatility in its financials have caused large price volatilities in the past, and I expect high – of further heightened – volatility in the near future with the ongoing takeover discussions. As such, I believe the current IV underestimate the true share price uncertainties and provides a favorable option setup for buyers. As a specific example, let me draw your attention to a call option with an expiration date of April 17, 2026, and a strike of $23. This option has an expiry after ZIM’s next ER scheduled on March 12, and thus can help accommodate surprises in the ER (due to earnings, takeover, or other geopolitical risks).

As seen, the option last traded with a premium of $1.65 per share only. The premium is about 7% of ZIM’s current share price of $22.4, allowing potential investors to express their bullish view with a relatively small capital layout compared to direct share ownership.

Also note that these NTM (near-the-money) options have attracted a sizable investors to help with liquidity. The option with a $23 strike has an open interest of more than 5,000 and a volume of 88 in the last trading session.

Other risks and final thoughts

To echo a point mentioned earlier, ZIM’s wild earnings swings is due to the fundamental of its business and is a structural risk that investors – either option traders or share owners – need to stomach. Looking past the quarterly fluctuations as shown above, the next chart shows its EPS estimates in the next 3 years. You see the same pattern here with extremely volatile year-over-year EPS changes. ZIM’s extreme EPS volatility is primarily a results of the fundamentals of the shipping sector, the sector’s sensitivity to geological events, and also the uniqueness of ZIM’s own business model. For example, ZIM’s earnings in recent quarters have been benefiting from the geopolitical disruptions – substantially in my view – in the Red Sea area. The disruptions have artificially constrained capacity and pushed freight rates higher, and even degree, outcome, and duration of the disruptions are ultimately unpredictable.

To compensate for the business uncertainties, ZIM is currently trading at exceptionally low valuation multiples compared to historical norms and industry peers. Specifically, the stock carries a TTM P/E ratio of only 2.7 and FWD ratio is only 8.26 as seen in the chart below. This represents a significant discount from the shipping industry average. However, due to the extreme volatility of its earnings, investors need to bear in mind the limited leading signal that its headline P/E ratios offer. But even in terms of price-to-book ratio, which is less affected by cyclical forces and/or geological risks, the stock is also attractively valued. As seen, its current P/B ratios sit at approximately 0.67 as of TTM and at 0.74 on an FWD basis, suggesting a sizable discount of its book value. Historically, the stock traded at an average P/B ratio of 1.46 in the past five years.

To wrap up, this ZIM follow-up is promoted mainly by two changes since my last writing: the takeover discussion and the changes of its IV In the option market. My assessment is that the current IV underestimates the true uncertainties underlying its business fundamentals and share prices. This discrepancy, when combined with the valuation multiples (especially in terms of book value discount), provides a favorable setup to go long on its call options.

data.title: Mondelez: Sweet Bargain For Dividend Investors
data.description:
It pays to be patient, especially when it comes to consumer staples stocks with durable brands. While near-term headwinds can cause price swings, patient investors can use market fear to their advantage by taking a long-term view. As Warren Buffett once aptly put it: “Successful investing takes time, discipline, and patience. No matter how great the talent or effort, some things just take time.”

This brings me to Mondelez International, Inc. (MDLZ), which I last covered in October, highlighting its robust top-line growth and diversified global portfolio of brands. MDLZ continues to trade in value territory at the current price of $55.09, which is close to its 52-week low of $51. It also carries a 3.6% dividend yield, as shown below.

In this article, I revisit MDLZ, including recent business results, and discuss why negative market sentiment on this stock has created a great buying opportunity for long-term value and income investors, so let’s get started!

Why MDLZ?

Mondelez is a global snacking giant with a presence in over 150 countries. Its portfolio of iconic brands includes Oreo, Ritz, Clif Bar, Cadbury Dairy Milk, and Toblerone chocolate. Over the trailing 12 months, MDLZ generated $37.6 billion in total revenue.

Those familiar with MDLZ may know that its results over the past few quarters have been affected by higher cocoa prices. This has led to margin and EPS pressure on the company. However, it's worth noting that the top line has continued to steadily grow since 2024 after a significant ramp-up in the years before that, as shown below.

MDLZ continued its recent track record of top-line growth during Q3 2025, with organic revenue rising by 3.4% YoY. This was driven by price increases, which more than offset a volume decline of 4.6% from the prior year period. This means that consumers were able to absorb at least some of the cost from higher cocoa prices. Emerging markets drove most of the growth, with organic revenue rising by 7.1% YoY, while developed markets organic revenue rose by just 1.2% YoY, as shown below.

MDLZ’s chocolate prices have risen by 30% over the past 12 reported months, and while demand has been relatively steady, management is seeing pockets of pressure. On the other hand, biscuit, cakes, and pastries demand has remained stable. This was particularly the case in Europe, where these products have all gained market share and volume growth.

Notably, price elasticity for MDLZ’s products in Europe was around 0.75, which is higher than management expectations of 0.4 to 0.5. For reference, a price elasticity of below 1 means that consumers are not as price sensitive, so the lower the score, the better. As such, MDLZ is working to address this issue by innovating into new flavors and formats while hitting on the right price points.

Looking ahead to Q4 results and beyond, management is guiding for full-year 2025 organic revenue growth of 4% and adjusted EPS decline of 15% from the prior year, due primarily to higher cocoa prices. Nonetheless, MDLZ still expects to maintain $3 billion in free cash flow for the full year, due in part to disciplined capital expense management. This would be on par with $3.055 billion in free cash flow generated in the prior year.

Moreover, MDLZ could see success in future zero-sugar offerings like what consumer staples giant Coca-Cola (KO) has seen with Coke Zero. This is reflected by MDLZ’s work in expanding the zero Oreo range as well as the gluten-free Oreo. Plus, protein continues to have its moment with consumers, and MDLZ is well-positioned with the Perfect Bar and BUILDERS, both of which have seen 20%+ growth.

Importantly, management expects to have passed the peak in cocoa prices, which means that it’s on track for better earnings growth in 2026. As shown below, cocoa futures have declined significantly and have dipped to $5,312 to start off the new year. While it still has a ways to go to reach the $2,500 level, the recent price actions are a move in the right direction.

MDLZ maintains a solid BBB credit rating from S&P. This is supported by $1.37 billion in cash on the balance sheet. While the net debt to TTM EBITDA ratio is moderately elevated at 3.66x, this has more to do with margin pressures from higher cocoa prices. With lower prices on the horizon, I would expect the leverage ratio to trend down this year closer to 3x.

Importantly for income investors, MDLZ currently yields 3.6%, and the dividend is well covered by a 67% payout ratio. The dividend also comes with a 5-year CAGR of 10.1% and 12 consecutive years of growth. As shown below, MDLZ’s TTM yield is at the highest point in at least 10 years.

Lastly, I find value in MDLZ at the current price of $55.09 with a forward PE of 19.0. While this valuation doesn’t scream bargain, it’s worth noting that this is because of the aforementioned expected decline in earnings last year. Analysts who follow the company expect a rebound in EPS this year and estimate 8% to 10% EPS growth annually over the next 4 years. As shown below, this could push the P/E ratio below 15 by 2028.

Plus, even as MDLZ’s forward P/E is elevated due to lower earnings in 2025, it still sits slightly below its historical P/E of 19.4, as shown below. As such, it appears the market is not baking in expectations for a turnaround in earnings this year.

Risks to MDLZ include its exposure to cocoa prices. Unforeseen structural cocoa shortages due to weather and crop disease in West Africa could have an outsized impact on the earnings outlook. Continued price hikes could result in more price elasticity risk as consumers trade down into other confectionery categories. Shifting consumer tastes away from packaged and sugary goods could pressure volume growth. Plus, a stronger U.S. dollar would translate to currency risk from MDLZ’s international revenues.

Investor Takeaway

Mondelez remains a high-quality consumer staples name with durable brands, global scale, and dependable cash flow, despite near-term earnings pressure from elevated cocoa costs. The market’s current pessimism has pushed the stock near multi-year lows and lifted the dividend yield to an attractive level. This is even as management maintains strong free cash flow and a solid balance sheet.

MDLZ’s growth is driven by emerging markets and innovation, and cocoa prices are showing signs of easing. As such, it appears positioned for an earnings rebound in 2026. For long-term value and income investors willing to look past short-term volatility, the current valuation offers a compelling entry point into a proven wealth compounder. For these reasons, I maintain a "Buy" rating on the stock.
